title: Pets and Mental Wellbeing
question: Do pets improve people's mental health and wellbeing?

styles.default:
Nowadays, millions of people keep pets as part of their daily lives. There is growing recognition that animals can significantly affect human health and happiness. I firmly believe that pets genuinely improve people's mental wellbeing.

I strongly believe that companionship is one of the greatest benefits pets provide. This is because animals offer unconditional affection and a constant, comforting presence. For example, elderly people living alone frequently report feeling far less isolated after getting a dog or cat. As a result, pet ownership significantly reduces feelings of depression and loneliness.

Furthermore, caring for a pet creates a meaningful sense of purpose and routine. This is because animals depend on their owners for food and exercise. For instance, walking a dog every morning gives owners a consistent daily structure. Consequently, people develop healthier habits and a stronger sense of responsibility.

Moreover, pets are widely known to relieve stress. This is because stroking an animal lowers blood pressure and reduces anxiety naturally. For example, hospitals increasingly use therapy animals to help patients cope. While some argue that pets are expensive, I believe these benefits clearly outweigh the costs.

In conclusion, I am convinced that pets have a genuinely positive impact on mental health. Overall, the companionship, routine and stress relief they provide make animals enormously valuable to human wellbeing.
styles.alternative1:
It is widely recognised that pet ownership has become an increasingly significant part of modern life. In recent times, researchers have paid growing attention to the link between animals and psychological health. From my perspective, pets make a meaningful contribution to people's wellbeing.

One key benefit of keeping a pet is the companionship it provides, helping to combat loneliness. This is because animals offer consistent emotional support without judgement. To illustrate, studies show that older adults with pets experience significantly lower rates of depression. Consequently, animals play a vital role in supporting emotional health.

Secondly, caring for a pet creates beneficial structure and purpose. This is because animals need regular feeding and exercise, encouraging owners to maintain healthy routines. As an example, dog owners who walk their pets daily naturally build physical activity into their lives. As a result, both physical and mental health improve together.

What is more, science confirms that animals effectively reduce stress. This is because interacting with pets triggers calming hormones such as oxytocin. For instance, therapy dog programmes in workplaces have produced measurable reductions in stress. Although critics highlight the costs of ownership, I would argue the mental health benefits justify these considerably.

To sum up, I strongly believe pets contribute enormously to human wellbeing. Taking everything into account, the companionship, routine and stress relief animals provide are of genuine and lasting value.
styles.alternative2:
In recent years, the connection between pet ownership and mental health has attracted growing interest from researchers and health professionals. It is common knowledge that many people form deep bonds with their animals. In my view, pets genuinely benefit people's psychological wellbeing.

The primary advantage of keeping a pet is the companionship it offers, reducing loneliness and distress. This is because animals provide love and loyalty people can rely upon consistently. For example, individuals living alone who own pets report higher levels of life satisfaction. Therefore, animals fill an important emotional role in people's lives.

Additionally, caring for a pet encourages healthy routines and a sense of purpose. This is because meeting an animal's daily needs gives owners structure and motivation to stay active. For instance, pet owners are more likely to maintain regular sleep patterns and exercise habits. Thus, caring for animals promotes broader lifestyle improvements.

Beyond this, spending time with pets is a proven method of managing stress. This is because even brief contact with animals measurably reduces cortisol levels. As an example, therapy animal programmes in hospitals show significant improvements in patients' emotional states. Despite arguments that pets are costly, I am convinced the psychological advantages far exceed any drawbacks.

In conclusion, I completely agree that pets improve people's mental health profoundly. On balance, the emotional support, purpose and stress relief animals provide represent enormous benefits to human happiness.

title: Sports at School
question: 'All students should have to do sport at school.' Do you agree?

styles.default:
It is widely recognised that physical education plays an important role in school life. In recent times, there has been considerable debate about whether sport should be compulsory for every student. I firmly believe that all students should be required to participate in sport at school.

To begin with, sport teaches essential teamwork and communication skills. This is because team games require players to cooperate and work towards shared goals. For example, playing football or basketball teaches young people to communicate effectively under pressure. As a result, students develop social skills that prove valuable throughout their lives.

Furthermore, the health benefits of regular exercise are enormous and well-documented. This is because sport strengthens the body, improves concentration and reduces the risk of serious illness. For instance, students who exercise regularly tend to perform better academically and experience lower rates of anxiety. Consequently, compulsory sport directly improves both physical and mental health.

However, some argue that forcing students who dislike sport creates unnecessary stress. While I understand this concern, schools can offer a variety of activities to suit different interests. For example, those who dislike team games might enjoy swimming or yoga instead. Therefore, compulsory sport need not mean a single activity for everyone.

In conclusion, I strongly believe sport should be mandatory for all students. Overall, the teamwork skills, health benefits and variety of options make compulsory physical education both fair and genuinely valuable.
styles.alternative1:
Nowadays, the role of physical education in schools generates significant debate among educators and parents. Few people would deny that exercise is critically important for young people's development. From my perspective, compulsory sport is an essential part of a well-rounded education.

Firstly, sport develops crucial skills in teamwork and collaboration. This is because organised games teach students to coordinate with others and resolve conflicts constructively. To illustrate, students in regular team sport frequently demonstrate stronger interpersonal skills in the classroom too. Consequently, the social benefits of school sport extend well beyond the playing field.

Secondly, physical activity delivers outstanding benefits for students' health and wellbeing. This is because exercise releases mood-enhancing chemicals while strengthening the cardiovascular system. As an example, schools that prioritise PE report lower student stress and improved academic engagement. As a result, mandatory sport directly supports overall development and achievement.

Although some students dislike sport, this can be addressed by offering diverse and inclusive activities. I would argue that when schools provide options like swimming or martial arts, virtually every student can find something they enjoy. For instance, inclusive programmes successfully engage students who previously felt excluded.

To sum up, I am convinced that compulsory school sport benefits all students. Taking everything into account, teamwork, health and inclusivity make physical education an indispensable part of school life.
styles.alternative2:
In recent years, mandatory physical education has become a growing topic of discussion in schools worldwide. It is common knowledge that young people today are increasingly inactive. In my view, requiring all students to participate in sport is an important and justified policy.

To start with, sport provides an excellent environment for developing teamwork and leadership. This is because cooperative games demand effective communication and mutual trust between participants. For example, students in school teams report improvements in their ability to work collaboratively in academic settings too. Therefore, sport cultivates transferable skills that benefit students across many areas of life.

Additionally, the health advantages of compulsory PE are profound and long-lasting. This is because regular childhood exercise establishes healthy habits that often continue into adulthood. For instance, physically active students are significantly less likely to develop obesity or heart disease later in life. Thus, mandatory sport is a valuable investment in long-term wellbeing.

On the other hand, critics claim that students who dislike sport should not be forced to participate. Nevertheless, I believe that offering a broad range of activities ensures everyone can engage meaningfully. As an example, dance, rock climbing and yoga appeal strongly to those who dislike traditional competitive sport.

In conclusion, I completely agree that sport should be compulsory for all students. On balance, the social skills, health benefits and inclusive options available make this an entirely reasonable requirement.

title: Advertising to Children
question: 'Advertisements which are aimed at children should not be allowed.' What do you think?
writingPoints: they can be entertaining and fun

styles.default:
Nowadays, children are exposed to a vast number of advertisements every day through television and social media. Because of this, there is considerable debate about whether marketing aimed at young people should be permitted. I firmly believe that such advertising causes more harm than good and should be banned.

Admittedly, some advertisements aimed at children are creative and genuinely entertaining. This is because companies produce engaging content with memorable characters that capture children's imagination. For example, animated adverts can be visually inventive and enjoyable to watch. However, entertainment value alone cannot justify the broader harmful effects of such advertising.

Furthermore, advertising aimed at children encourages unnecessary spending. This is because young children are particularly susceptible to persuasive marketing techniques. For instance, children who see adverts for toys frequently pressure their parents into purchases they cannot afford. Consequently, such advertising creates significant financial stress for many families.

Most importantly, advertising has a damaging psychological impact on young people. This is because children lack the critical thinking skills needed to recognise manipulative messages. For example, adverts promoting unhealthy foods contribute directly to poor dietary choices and childhood obesity. Therefore, targeted advertising causes real and measurable harm to children's development.

In conclusion, I strongly believe that advertising aimed at children should not be allowed. Overall, the financial pressure, psychological manipulation and health consequences far outweigh any entertainment value it may provide.
styles.alternative1:
In recent times, the question of whether children should be exposed to targeted advertising has become increasingly important. It is widely recognised that young people are among the most vulnerable audiences for commercial marketing. From my perspective, advertising directed at children is harmful and should be prohibited.

It is true that some advertisements designed for children are imaginative and enjoyable. This is because companies create visually stimulating content with music and characters that appeal to young audiences. To illustrate, many children's adverts are remembered fondly by adults who grew up watching them. Despite this, the entertainment value does not outweigh the negative consequences.

Moreover, advertisements aimed at children promote unnecessary consumption and pressure family budgets. This is because children cannot yet evaluate advertising critically or distinguish wants from genuine needs. As an example, children bombarded with toy adverts make persistent demands that parents find very difficult to refuse. As a result, family finances suffer unnecessarily.

Above all, the psychological effects of advertising on children are deeply concerning. This is because young minds are highly impressionable and easily manipulated by sophisticated marketing. For instance, repeated exposure to fast food adverts has been directly linked to unhealthy eating habits. Consequently, advertising causes measurable damage to children's development.

To sum up, I am convinced that advertising targeting children should be banned. Taking everything into account, the harm caused by manipulation, financial pressure and poor health outcomes is far too significant to ignore.
styles.alternative2:
Over recent years, concerns about advertising targeting children have grown considerably among parents, health professionals and governments worldwide. Few people would deny that children are a uniquely vulnerable consumer audience. In my view, advertisements aimed at children should not be permitted.

It must be acknowledged that certain advertisements for children are visually inventive and fun to watch. This is because advertisers use bright colours, music and appealing characters to create engaging content. For example, some children's adverts have become part of popular culture due to their creativity. Nevertheless, I believe that these qualities cannot excuse the serious problems such advertising creates.

In addition, child-targeted advertising drives unnecessary purchases and places pressure on families. This is because young children cannot critically assess commercial messages directed at them. For instance, children who see toy adverts regularly become persistent consumers whose demands parents feel obligated to satisfy. Thus, advertising creates real financial tension within households.

Most seriously, advertising exploits children's psychological vulnerability in damaging ways. This is because children's brains have not yet developed the reasoning needed to resist manipulation. As an example, research shows that advertising contributes directly to childhood obesity by normalising unhealthy food choices. As a result, long-term consequences for children's health can be severe.

In conclusion, I completely agree that advertising aimed at children should be banned. On balance, the psychological harm, financial pressure and health risks make it impossible to justify.

title: Technology and the Future
question: Do you think technology will improve our lives in the future?
writingPoints: artificial intelligence and health (your own idea)

styles.default:
Nowadays, technology is advancing at a pace that would have been unimaginable to previous generations. Because of this, many people debate whether these changes will ultimately improve human life. I firmly believe that technology will continue to improve our lives in several profound ways.

To begin with, technology will make communication and global connection easier than ever before. This is because digital tools already allow people to share ideas instantly across vast distances. For example, future virtual reality developments could allow people to attend family events from anywhere on earth. As a result, geographical distance will become a far less significant challenge worldwide.

Furthermore, technology has the potential to transform employment positively. This is because automation will eliminate dangerous and repetitive work, freeing people to pursue fulfilling careers. For instance, robots already perform hazardous factory tasks, protecting workers from injury. Consequently, future workers could enjoy considerably safer and more rewarding professional lives.

In addition, advances in artificial intelligence promise to revolutionise healthcare. This is because AI systems already detect certain diseases more accurately than trained doctors. For example, early cancer detection using AI could save millions of lives annually. While some worry that technology creates problems, I believe the health benefits alone justify great optimism.

In conclusion, I am convinced that technology will significantly improve human lives going forward. Overall, better communication, safer employment and revolutionary healthcare make the technological future genuinely exciting.
styles.alternative1:
In recent times, rapid technological progress has generated both excitement and anxiety about the future. Few people would deny that technology has already transformed modern life beyond recognition. From my perspective, the technological future is overwhelmingly positive and full of genuine promise.

Firstly, technology will dramatically enhance our ability to communicate globally. This is because emerging tools such as virtual reality and real-time translation will remove barriers of distance and language. To illustrate, within a generation, people separated by thousands of miles may interact as naturally as if in the same room. Consequently, human relationships across the globe will become richer and more accessible.

Secondly, technological development will reshape employment in ways that benefit workers significantly. This is because automation will take over unpleasant and dangerous tasks currently performed by people. As an example, self-driving vehicles are already reducing risks faced by transport workers daily. As a result, people will be freed to focus on more rewarding forms of work.

Moreover, advances in artificial intelligence will transform healthcare and save countless lives. This is because AI analyses vast medical data far more quickly than any human specialist. For instance, personalised treatments tailored to individual genetics are already becoming reality. Although concerns about technology are understandable, I would argue these medical advances alone represent an extraordinary improvement in human life.

To sum up, I strongly believe technology will make our lives considerably better ahead. Taking everything into account, improvements in communication, employment and healthcare point convincingly towards a brighter future.
styles.alternative2:
Over recent decades, technological innovation has accelerated at an extraordinary pace, reshaping nearly every aspect of human existence. It is widely recognised that this rate of change shows no sign of slowing. In my view, this relentless progress will bring enormous benefits to people worldwide.

To start with, future technology will make communication faster and more inclusive than ever before. This is because innovations in connectivity will allow anyone, anywhere, to communicate effortlessly with anyone else. For example, real-time language translation is already reducing barriers between cultures. Therefore, technology will bring people closer together in previously unimaginable ways.

Additionally, technological progress promises better and safer working conditions worldwide. This is because intelligent machines will take over tasks that are gruelling or genuinely dangerous for humans. For instance, mining robots already spare workers from the most hazardous environments. Thus, automation will protect human health and dignity in the workplace.

Beyond this, artificial intelligence combined with medical science could eliminate many diseases causing enormous suffering. This is because AI diagnostic tools are advancing with remarkable speed and precision. As an example, AI has already matched specialist doctors in diagnosing certain cancers from medical scans. Despite concerns about risks, I am convinced these breakthroughs represent a future worth embracing confidently.

In conclusion, I completely agree that technology will profoundly improve human life. On balance, gains in communication, workplace safety and healthcare make progress one of humanity's greatest sources of hope.

title: Electric Cars and the Future
question: Why are electric cars becoming more popular, and how might this trend affect the future?
writingPoints: improvements in battery technology (your own idea)

styles.default:
In recent years, electric cars have become an increasingly common sight on roads worldwide. There is growing recognition that this shift is one of the most significant changes in transport in over a century. I believe that the rise of electric vehicles will have a profoundly positive impact on our future.

To begin with, growing concern about climate change has driven enormous interest in cleaner alternatives to petrol cars. This is because conventional vehicles produce harmful emissions that contribute directly to global warming and urban pollution. For example, cities such as London have reported measurably improved air quality where electric vehicles have replaced conventional traffic. As a result, the environmental case for electric cars is now widely accepted.

Furthermore, government policies and financial incentives have played a crucial role in accelerating adoption. This is because many governments offer tax reductions and subsidies to encourage consumers to switch. For instance, several European countries have announced plans to ban new petrol car sales within the next decade. Consequently, electric vehicles are rapidly becoming the mainstream choice globally.

In addition, rapid improvements in battery technology have made electric cars far more practical. This is because modern batteries charge faster and last considerably longer than before. For example, some models now travel over five hundred kilometres on a single charge. Therefore, the practical barriers that once discouraged buyers are quickly disappearing.

In conclusion, I am convinced that the rise of electric cars will transform transport and benefit the planet greatly. Overall, environmental necessity, supportive policies and better technology make electric vehicles the obvious choice for the future.
styles.alternative1:
Nowadays, electric vehicles have moved from a futuristic novelty to a practical reality embraced by millions of drivers. It is widely recognised that this transformation is reshaping the global automotive industry at remarkable speed. From my perspective, the growing popularity of electric cars is an enormously positive development for both people and the planet.

Firstly, deepening concern about climate change has made electric vehicles an increasingly attractive choice. This is because combustion engines are a major source of harmful carbon emissions and urban air pollution. To illustrate, studies in cities with low-emission zones show clear and consistent reductions in harmful pollutants. Consequently, the environmental benefits of switching to electric transport are both urgent and demonstrable.

Secondly, government intervention has proven instrumental in making electric vehicles accessible to more people. This is because purchase grants, reduced taxes and expanding charging networks lower barriers for ordinary consumers. As an example, Norway's generous incentive scheme has resulted in electric cars representing the majority of new vehicle sales nationally. As a result, strong policy support has proven enormously effective at accelerating this transition.

Moreover, continuing battery advances are eliminating the remaining obstacles to widespread adoption. This is because each new generation offers greater range, faster charging and lower costs. For instance, solid-state batteries in development promise to dramatically outperform current technology. Although concerns about infrastructure remain, I would argue the direction of travel is clearly towards a fully electric future.

To sum up, I strongly believe electric vehicles will bring significant benefits to transport and the environment. Taking everything into account, environmental pressure, government support and technological progress make electric cars the transport solution of the future.
styles.alternative2:
Over recent years, electric cars have grown from an expensive curiosity into a mainstream mode of transport used by millions worldwide. Few people would deny that this transformation is changing how societies think about travel and environmental responsibility. In my view, the increasing popularity of electric vehicles is one of the most encouraging trends of the modern era.

To start with, the desire to address climate change has made electric cars a compelling alternative to conventional vehicles. This is because fossil fuel cars are among the leading causes of carbon dioxide emissions globally. For example, widespread electric vehicle adoption could reduce transport-related emissions by more than half within two decades. Therefore, choosing electric is increasingly seen as an environmental responsibility, not merely a preference.

Additionally, proactive government policies have significantly accelerated consumer adoption. This is because financial incentives and investment in charging infrastructure make electric cars a far more practical proposition. For instance, countries such as the Netherlands have built extensive fast-charging networks, removing the anxiety once associated with electric ownership. Thus, effective policy has been a decisive factor in this global shift.

Furthermore, battery technology innovation continues to make electric cars better and cheaper every year. This is because sustained research investment has produced batteries with significantly greater range and longevity. As an example, average electric car range has more than doubled over the past decade. While raw material challenges remain, I am convinced a fully electric future is both inevitable and desirable.

In conclusion, I completely agree that the rise of electric cars is a positive and transformative trend. On balance, environmental benefits, government backing and better technology are making electric vehicles the dominant transport choice for the decades ahead.
